item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
TrustTheFungus

+1

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Timer Expired. Edits made as discussed below. Author may revert at any time.

The WikiWalk Team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team.

+ show block
– hide block

The first article this reminded me of was SCP-1909, which implies that Alexander the Great was at least partially anomalous, and that the name Alexander may be anomalous. As such, I propose the following:

Changing:

Blue monkeys shit shrimp. University the [illegible] makes Babylon, which shall fall. Our laughing explodes into the universe at the speed of Alexandria. Negative 48/17/6 the ides paid their fines.Alex Alexander Alex Al Al friend Al al al al al al alcohol alchemist algebra blame the pork. We have bone scouted trough this plain. value of authorities capacity

To:

Blue monkeys shit shrimp. University the [illegible] makes Babylon, which shall fall. Our laughing explodes into the universe at the speed of Alexandria. Negative 48/17/6 the ides paid their fines.Alex Alexander Alex Al Al friend Al al al al al al alcohol alchemist algebra blame the pork. We have bone scouted trough this plain. value of authorities capacity

Additionally, this SCP mentions the Library of Alexandria, and heavily implies that it is from there. This is supported by comments made by ihp on The Leak. It just so happens that the Foundation has librarian from Alexandria in Subject 911-1.

Changing this:

Possible link to the Library of Alexandria, which some sources say was partially destroyed by Julius Caesar around 48 B.C.E.

To this:

Possible link to the Library of Alexandria, which some sources say was partially destroyed by Julius Caesar around 48 B.C.E. Consulting Subject 911-1 about the possible origins of SCP-1658 has been proposed.

If anyone has any other idea, feel free to suggest them below.

item.title: Re: Image Update
item.content:encoded: It must be done, but I will miss the previous picture used here.

item.title: Image Update
item.content:encoded:
Might as well do this pre-emptively; due to the fact that Staff is going to update their image rules and I can't find anything about the original image other that it wans't CC-Compliant, I've made a new image for the article.

New image is by me, released under CC license.

Translations and Spoilers
– hide block

From Top-right going clockwise, translated from bad Polish:

Read between the lines

The dragon has burned the books, now we burn the dragon's lungs

We just want to go home home home home home

(Upside-down) Don't say that you love me

(Immediately above that) TUSK! (Yes, I was listening to Fleetwood Mac while making this. Sue me.)

Vladimir Son of the Dragon

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Now I wonder who writes such a thing on the walls of a house?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Knowing this might ruin your experience with this SCP, but…

the text written in the image is an action novel (of dubious quality), involving knife fights, street gangsters and leg sweep moves.

Now I'm thinking how do you fit that into the context here…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: "Blue monkeys shit shrimp" made me laugh for a solid minute)))

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This this this this

Otherwise… I didn't really feel this. I quite liked it (CS rage aside) until the human test bit. I don't understand why it reacted how it did.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It took me a minute to make the connection. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Did I get the right vibe from this when I say that the books express themselves, rage and cry over their fallen (burned) comrades?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I know this is late, but the strikethrough markup does not carry through line breaks.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This pushes all of the right buttons for me, being the linguistics and language nerd I am.

Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
MTF-Phi-12 ("Reading Rainbows")

Great, now all I can hear is the theme song.

Take a look, it's in a book, Reading RAINBOOOOOWWW!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Marvelous. +1. And welcome to the triple digits!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What system of rules is there? Binary is essentially an arbitrary string of zeroes and ones. Without a proper reading frame, set bitlength, and encoding system, it means absolutely nothing. In the end, it would just be a random string in a base 2 number system. It's the same as me typing a string of random numbers in base 10.

111001. That's 57 in base 10 unsigned. -6 in two's complement notation. -25 in sign-magnitude notation. It's "9" if interpreted using ASCII. Its an opcode for an assembly function, as well as countless other possible mappings.

Binary is no more a language than decimal is.

Anyways, all that aside, upvoted. I'm not sure why, but the article sent a chill down my back near the end. Though the mention of binary as a language upsets me as a programmer.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Addendum 1658-01 is what made me find this so interesting. It's like revolutionary text. It has a hivemind with all other samples of itself, and apparently with every piece of literature ever written.

Also, reading all of that unstable text is making me view every other sentence as if it's a grammatical trainwreck.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like this, but I do have a couple of nitpicks. First of all, the information on the SCP describes it as forming "gibberish", yet there is a clear and consistent meaning to all the phrases, rambling and vaguely incoherent though it may be. Foundation personnel aren't stupid, and it seems a bit unlikely that they would miss the connections here, especially given that a number of links were listed in notations in the provided material. Secondly, and tying into the first, is the fact that the pattern leads to a pretty clear conclusion, specifically that the organism in question is both sapient and hostile, using its toxic properties as a targeted attack against those who destroy written knowledge. By the end of the article, it also appears that the testing and containment procedures used by the Foundation have made them a target.

In light of these, the Euclid classification seems questionable. Not necessarily wrong, but it is definitely questionable. The current description of the Keter classification doesn't really fit, but I remember an old explanation stating that it applied to things that posed a specific and targeted threat to human life. Under that definition, 1658 would at least warrant a proposal from one the researchers assigned to reclassify as Keter in an addendum. Backing that is its apparent awareness of, and hostility toward, the Foundation itself. Its ability to propagate, possibly spontaneously, within secure archives, and desire to take vengeance for destruction of test materials would make it feasible that it might pose a risk of compromising other containment procedures, which, while it might be dismissed by higher-level staff, would certainly be of concern to the people whose daily job it is to be paranoid and consider every possibility while dealing with strange and dangerous SCPs.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
On the contrary, I believe the definition you've provided shows why binary isn't a language. Binary satisfies some of the definition. It is a set of symbols: ones and zeros. It does have rules, such as the number of bits per character and the layout of the ASCII table (both of which are technically outside the scope of binary itself and all to do with which implementation you're using). But binary inherits every shred of meaning from the language it's encoding. The symbols only encode meaning by reference to the original.

Binary is a system of representation, like if I decided to write a note in cursive or in all capital letters or to encrypt the note in a numerical code. Actually, that's the best analogy because that's exactly what you're doing when "translating" a text to binary: the ones and zeros are added up in specific chunks to form a single number, which corresponds to a specific character (a letter or punctuation mark or so on) in a table. Is Morse code a language? No; it's a code. Binary is no different from a super-secret encoder spy ring offered on the back of a Wondertainment brochure.

*shrug* This really is a pedantic nitpick, as you pointed out. I guess it's up to the author of the entry.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
One sec, let me find my pedantic nerd glasses…ah-ha! there we go.

"Language" (noun) - a system of communication with a set of symbols and rules which can be used to convey meaning between individuals.

Binary seems to fit.

This concludes the test of the emergency nerd deployment system. Please return to your regularly scheduled browsing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So… binary is not a language, at least not in the common sense. There are set tables for translating Unicode (ASCII or UTF) to/from a binary representation, but it's a direct substitution with nothing to do with meaning or syntax. I could use binary to write English, just as I could use it to write Javanese or Basque or Pig Latin or a string of punctuation marks, non-printing characters, and even sound commands.

Because it's just one and zeroes by definition, a frame shift error in reading would make understanding a binary phrase difficult, but this would be a fairly trivial challenge for any cryptologist, computer programmer, or information scientist so long as the source language is a known one and the "translation" were using a standard unicode <-> binary table. More difficult would be the translation into English from the source language, because that's subjective, not an objective substitution of one way of writing (that language's native writing system) for another representation (ones and zeroes).

Regardless, cool idea. It was a bit generic at first but I really like the anti-censorship angle.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
—I wonder if this fungus is at all related to a certain University (GoI).

If so, the fungus is probably right: Knowledge IS power, and the "Alexandria University" alternate timeline seems to prove it.—

EDIT:

Dear god how did I screw that up.

Also, why is the syntax for "strikethrough" not working?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I just realized how wonky a lot of these translations are; some of the languages these are translated from don't even have capital letters, and yet….

ALEXANDRIA

So, I've updated them to be a bit more realistic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
See now, I enjoy this.
Also I don't see it as only a fungus that hates the destruction of knowledge, but even the "souls" of the books trapped in the Library of Alexandria. It seems that they were so angry about the destruction that they literally went insane within 50 years and began infecting pieces of literature about to be destroyed/copies of literature that had been destroyed in the past.
Was that intentional, because I haven't seen anyone say anything about it?
If it wasn't I just made it a whole bunch creepier for myself. 0.0

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This reminds me a lot of SCP-1782, what with the whole "anomalous thing that got made even stranger due to some traumatic incident" angle.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Indeed. I'm about to type a sentence I never would have expected to write.

I'm rooting for the fungus.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The heavy handed anti-censorship angle kills this for me, as well as the implication that the fungus is angry about the infection test as if it has never infected someone who couldn't get treated before.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Interesting. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Congrats!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
.__.

Wat.
How.

How is this a featured article.

I.

WOW.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Knowledge is power.
Every knowledge.
All knowledge.

Knowledge is power.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I seem to be in the minority here, but I'm just not feeling it.

Sentient fungus which loves knowledge and is poorly contained, and outraged.

Yes, it's now targeting animals.

I feel like I'm still waiting for the hook. Am I missing something?
Downvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked this one! Especially the outrage it seemed to express at the end. Upvoted!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is good. Upvoted. Noticed a few things:

"Photography has shown that the "actual" appearance of SCP-1658 resembles several languages, the most common including Binary, Mandarin Chinese, Greek,and English."

Missing a space in the italicized portion.

"Documents have since been destroyed with backups made from digital copies."

The sentence suggests that the documents were destroyed using the backups - a rather odd thing to do. I think you meant "since been replaced" or something similar.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Upvoted at the correct use of the word "trichothecene," and never found any reason to take it back.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very nicely done. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Okay, yeah, this one gets really, really good toward the end. I honestly went from "meh, seems done" to "ooh" and shivers by the end of the addenda.

One concern: even if it's not actually the same picture, the image for this article strongly reminds me of the one for that crazy Russian guy who can't stop writing down what the aliens put in his brain.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So amazing. I never thought I'd see so much personality and pathos from a fungus.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I didn't even bother to finish reading the rest of the article before upvoting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I had an idea just like this one. You clever bastard.

Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Loved that +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
MTF-Phi-12 ("Reading Rainbows")

Instantly upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The addendum is powerful in this article. And like Michael Atreus, the picture reminds me of something I've seen before.

Also,

"IS THIS OUR FATE"

All materials subsequently burned. Following burning, an outbreak of SCP-1658 was found in the Foundation Archives, contaminated over █████ original documents with the message "WE WILL NOT STAND FOR THIS". Documents have since been destroyed with backups made from digital copies.

I like this part.

Well done! +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: very interesting and creative i like it

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Figured out the hook partway through the addendum.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Fixed a bunch of typos a while ago, forgot to put it in rev history.

Also, don't forget to remove the picture from the file.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is glorious. Seemingly sentient hive-minded fungus that hates the destruction of knowledge. Having written an SCP myself that WANTS to destroy knowledge, the counterpoint (unintentional I'm sure) is delicious.

Also:

For all of our brother POWERKNOWLEDGE who have joined us, avenge yourself, for you were been not-murdered.

That may be one of the greatest sentences ever written.

EDIT: Made note readable. Spaces are important!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's ok. Certainly solidly written enough. I'm neither for nor against it, I guess. It's kind of funny that anti-censorship mold would populate written materials where it would potentially obscure the writing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I rented a room in a filthy frathouse one summer in college, about 17 years ago. The picture instantly reminded me of what I saw when I first walked in: black writing all over the dingy white walls. It took three coats to hide; I could not paint over the disconnected ramblings fast enough. The community garbage can lived in the men's room which had broken windows without screens - by trash day every week, it was 10% maggots by weight. Two months later, lightning struck the building during a storm; the attic burned for an hour or so before the campus fire brigade put it out. Two days later, I and about 20 other renters were escorted by police to our rooms to collect our stuff. The fire, smoke and water damage didn't seem too bad (no damage to the occupied floors) but the building was condemned: I couldn't help but think there was more "unfitness" revealed by the post-fire inspection than we were ever aware of. This SCP brings all that back with an exclamation point. Kudos. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No need to be gentle. Thank you to everyone in chat who commented on this.
